speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 21, 1995. The survivor is Leon Ginsburg. The interview is Sheila Scharfman. We are here in Tappan, Rockland County, USA. And we're speaking in English. You guys. Today is December 21, 1995. I'm here with Leon Ginsburg. My name is Sheila Scharfman. We're in Tappan, Rockland County, USA, and we're speaking in English. Can you please tell us and spell for us your name and your name as it was at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My name is Leon Ginsburg-- G-I-N-S-B-U-R-G. In Europe it was spelt with a Z, G-I-N-Z-B-U-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have any other names, and can you please spell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. During the war when I lived as a Catholic, my name was Stanislaw-- that's S-T-A-N-I-S-L-A-W-- Kwiatkowski. And that's a difficult one. K-W-I-A-T-K-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birthdate?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my birthdate is 6/12/1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm now 63.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the city and country of your birth? And if you could spell those.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was born in Poland in the-- an area called Volhynia. And it's like part of the Ukraine. Now it's an independent Ukrainian state. And the name of the town was Maciejow. That's M-A-C-I-E-J-O-W. The. Ukrainians now renamed it Lukov, L-U-K-O-V.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was Poland up to 1939. When the Germans invaded Poland, there was a pact between Hitler and Stalin where Russia was going to get the eastern part of Poland, which is the Ukraine. So this area fell under the Russian occupation in 1939 until 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Can you tell us about your life as a boy in Maciejow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our town was a-- it was an Orthodox Jewish town. I would say 60% or 70% of the population was Jewish. And we were surrounded by villages which were mostly Ukrainian and perhaps some Polish Catholic colonies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the town served in a supporting role in all the crafts to support the farmers. And so, for instance, the-- the tailors, and the shoemakers, and the cabinet makers, and the harness makers, and all-- the blacksmiths-- all the crafts would be performed in the town to support the surrounding communities. Where in this town did you live? What kind of neighborhood was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in like almost the center of the town. And there were-- there were streets. The-- the streets were-- were cobble stones. And we had sidewalks. And there were some areas that were residential, just homes. And then in the center of the town you had the-- where the stores were. And the marketplace was near the stores. And on a certain-- certain day of the week, all the farmers that wanted to do business in town would come down with horse and wagon and park in that market area. And they would be selling their products to people in town that wanted to buy them, from fruits, to milk, and butter, and grain, and trading horses and other livestock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you live with? Who was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of house did you live in and who did you live with?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a house. And I lived with my mother. And her name was Pesel. And my sister, who was the oldest, her name was Blimele, [? Blime. ?] And a brother, Herschel [? or ?] [? Zvi. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And a grandmother, Bela, Bela Goldberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was-- what was your life like in this village, you as a little boy in this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a town. It was a little town--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a town.

speaker: interviewee
text: --actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before they war did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, I was already went through Polish kindergarten and probably in first grade, started in first grade. And that was like half a day. And the other half a day, we went for Hebrew studies. It was a-- it was a a Talmud Torah, which was a combination of yeshiva-- actually, it was more of a yeshiva than-- than a Hebrew school because there was already competition between the younger modern generation, who were embracing Zionism and who wanted to learn Hebrew, in fact use Hebrew as a speaking language instead of Yiddish, which was spoken in most homes. So we would learn basically the religious aspect of Judaism there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say Orthodox was, in that town, as conservative Judaism was in this area. Everybody was sort of Orthodox. Although there was already a difference between the older generation that still wore beards and [? payot ?] and some of the Hasidic garb. The younger generation already dressed in a modern way with suits and ties and were-- and didn't have any [? payot ?] anymore and didn't wear beards.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do for-- for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents owned a store that sold material for suits, and coats, and dresses, like a garment type of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who worked in that store?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-- my mother worked in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your brother and sister go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and sister went to school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember visiting that store? Did you visit it often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As a matter of fact, on that market day, which I believe was-- it was either Wednesday or Thursday, it would get very crowded, a lot of customers. And then my mother would ask us to come and help, like just to be in the store to make sure that-- you know, cause the shelves were open, people don't take things and walk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this particular community where you lived was really predominately Jewish or was it mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I would say the center of the town and going outside towards the suburbs a little bit was mostly Jewish. And then starting from there, the farms would start already. So it would be people that live in town but actually have land outside of the city and-- but they would have their livestock in-- in-- in the-- in the [? CLO-- ?] in that area, in the barns. So that would start already being non-Jewish. And that would be mostly Ukrainian. Because that area of Poland was-- was considered the Ukraine. And I would say 80% of the population was Ukrainian Orthodox-- Russian or Ukrainian Orthodox. And about 10% of the population was Polish Catholic. And then 10% of the overall population was 10% Jewish. But in this particular town, probably 60% to 70% of the population was Jewish because all the crafts were concentrated there and the stores were concentrated in town. And the Jews lived in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a little boy going to school, do you remember feeling any sense of being different or not because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being that, in a way, the majority in-- in-- in school was Jewish because of the concentration of population there, we-- we did not feel different from that sense. Being that this was under Polish control, all of the official posts, like the post office and the-- and the police, it was all controlled by Polish Catholics, including all the teachers were Polish Catholics.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a little uneasiness from that point of view, because there was-- especially with from the nationalistic Polish population-- there was a sense of antisemitism. And during that particular period, Hitler was already doing his thing in-- in Germany. And information was feeding back to Poland quite rapidly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was already an organized Polish antisemitism in some of the extreme parties, especially the right. There were some extreme right parties that were beginning to-- to organize boycotts on Jewish stores, although not in our town in particular. But that was already the going things in other parts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a child, you didn't really sense antisemitism in you town?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a lesser extent, because the-- the non-Jewish boys would-- would-- especially the Polish boys-- would tend to look down at the, even though we were in a sense a majority in town, as second class citizens in a way. For some reason, the Polish mind never saw a Jew as being a Polish citizen per se. And they would always-- and any-- if they wanted to taunt us or in any way in insulting, they would always say Zydzi do Palestyny, Jews to Palestine. They knew we didn't belong there for some reason, that we should leave in other words and go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a little boy did you ever play with Christian children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much, although with neighbors like-- there-- my grandfather lived on a street that was already more mixed. And so we would go into their houses once in a while. But not much really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you describe the atmosphere, if you could, of your town? Was it basically a happy place? Was it-- was it-- were people struggling? I know you were young, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was a hard-working community. Even the people that owned stores and owned businesses, and they you could say were 'well off,' quote, they were-- they still worked very hard. The people in the crafts, they worked all day in their shops. But life was very happy, especially when the Sabbath came around. And everything was closed. All the stores would be closed on the Sabbath. And that possibly was a little bit of a friction type of situation where they probably-- the non-Jews would probably look at this as, look, Saturday I want to go and buy something and all the stores are closed. But everybody was so religious that they wouldn't dare-- even though they weren't so religious wouldn't dare keep stores open on-- on-- on Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Saturday was like a holiday. And everybody, at least all the men, all the males would go to synagogue. And they would dress up in their Sabbath clothes. And even if they owned one suit, they would put it on on-- on that day and they would look prosperous. And everybody would come to synagogue clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and the synagogue, besides the praying atmosphere, was in a way a social get together type of atmosphere. And I had my friends there and we used to get together. Usually like between prayers when we could get away, we would go behind a certain area and tell stories and go during-- when it was warm go outside and play games and then come back into the synagogue and, you know, continue praying. The atmosphere in the synagogue was Orthodox, very Orthodox. Everybody wore long [? talesim. ?] And-- and it was a very sincere type of atmosphere of praying.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you-- do you remember knowing or feeling that something was changing? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1939 when the war broke out, when Germany attacked Poland, we didn't have much military activity in our town. A few planes passed by and they dropped a few bombs, actually fell outside of the city. So we did not get much of a direct military activity there. And being that our area of Poland was going to end up on the Russian-- it was a vacuum for a while. For about a week, the Polish authorities disappeared and the Russians didn't come in yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what-- what you or your mother may have talked about? Do you remember what did you think was happening at that point? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that the war broke out and the Germans attacked Poland. I still saw some of the remnant Polish army, like they were disorganized and panicky, didn't know really where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they tried to get away from the Germans, so they-- they were running east. They ended up in our town. And it was the type of military-- basically horse and wagons and pulling some artillery pieces. Because the Polish army was not mechanized--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see them--

speaker: interviewee
text: --much

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were in the streets or?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were on the streets, yeah. They were all over on the streets in a way sitting and taking breaks like in-between continuing to go somewhere else. But there was actually nowheres to go because what-- I don't know if they knew it or not, but the Russians started coming in, going towards the west. And eventually, of course, they had to-- either they just disappeared, changed clothes, or-- or they would be taken prisoners--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what did it mean to you as a little boy when you saw the Polish soldiers on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I-- I knew very little about history and geography. So it-- it really-- it really-- I knew things were happening. It didn't really mean that much at that point, you know? I mean, you could sense danger because these soldiers running and not being sure what's happening. They could shoot at any moment. You could see their faces were stern and angry. And so it was a dangerous situation from that point of view.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother say to you, be careful, or did she give you any special--

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it didn't happen, you know, for a long time. So like it was a very short period where these soldiers passed by. And perhaps-- perhaps they were beginning to realize that maybe politically what was happening. I'm sure they must have gotten some information that the Russians were going to come into that area. And I would say they just-- most of them just melted away into the population, probably changed clothes. And so it was a slow period, a short period of time that this happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day or the days that the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember seeing-- there were-- there were a few communists in town. And they knew already what was happening. And they weren't prepared. They didn't-- probably didn't know and found out later, because they started making placards, you know. I remember white sheets. They took white sheets. And they were painting it with red ink, like welcoming-- welcoming the Russian-- the Russian army. But for about a week, nobody came. So the town was in a way like a vacuum for about a week. And then suddenly the Russian army started coming in, most of it passing our town and going towards Lubon, closer to the border, where the new border-- where the river Bug was located. And that was going to be the new border between Russia and Germany. So they were heading in that direction. And eventually a division settled in our town and-- and the surrounding areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians came in, did you still go to school? Did anything change for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then of course the schools were then changed. I remember now going to school in what used to be the Talmud Torah now became the Russian school where most of us went to. Now I'm not sure what happened to the other school, whether it was used or not. But we went to this new school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in this new school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were learning math and Russian and starting to emphasize some Russian poets. Like one particular was-- was a Ukrainian poet. His name escapes me. And I remember the poem of his. And when I came back recently to the Ukraine to put up a monument, they asked me what I remembered. And-- and for some reason I mentioned that poem, which was in Ukrainian. And they were so impressed that I remembered that poem that they printed it in the newspaper the next day. And they-- they didn't say much about-- about who I was, and why I was there, and that I'd lost a whole family, but that poem made the headlines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you and your friends sort of-- were you-- were you-- were you sort of taken aback or surprised by these changes? Or did you kind of just go with the flow? What was the feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just-- you know, there was-- for us kids, it wasn't a main-- it wasn't a big change except the school. And incidentally, that poet was Taras Shevchenko, Ukrainian well-known poet. So we-- we went to school. And in a way, you know, it was like normal. There was a lot of other activity going on where now the stores-- Russians didn't believe in private enterprise. So-- and most people in the town were involved in crafts, and-- and stores, and commerce. So now suddenly everything died in a way. My mother managed to get some of the material out of the store before-- because when the Russians came in, they went through the stores and bought everything. Whatever was inside, they bought. So she managed to get out some of her goods from the store. So subsequently we were able to sell it like, you know, to-- to special customers that my mother used to deal with. And so that gave us a way of making a living. Otherwise, it became very tough for people that were only involved in commerce. There was some other jobs formed where areas where Jews couldn't have jobs before, like all of the official government, like the post office and the-- and-- and the government offices and-- and schools, and so on, now some Jews could get jobs in those areas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you sister and brother doing at this time? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they continued going to school also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the next big thing that you kind of remember in terms of a change happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the next big thing that happens is in-- in June of 1941 when Germany attacks Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about this in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, that-- that was happening very fast. Because a lot of Russian military soldiers and officers were stationed in our town, being that it was so close. We were about maybe 50 miles or less from the border. So that area they had a lot of bases there and stationed army. They used to train there. And my grandparents had a big house. So part of it was used by the Russian high officers to-- they-- they brought their families down. So they lived in-- in the house. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: They lived in your grandparents' house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- they could do that. They would requisition either part of a house or-- or half a house. So they had like two rooms in a house that they were using. So we-- we knew. They started packing and getting out of there fast, so. And we saw the military activity. And basically, the activity was that the Russians running away, you know, running back east. Because the German attack was so well-organized, and motorized, mechanized. And-- and the attack was a surprise. Russia didn't-- didn't expect it at that particular time so that was very little-- there was very little resistance. It just moved fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel happening in the town as this was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was a feeling of fright. Because the streets were empty. And there were Russian sentries stationed like in the center of the town to help direct traffic, and very, very serious and very stern. I remember seeing a truck. And actually, I shouldn't have been. It was like at the center of town where the sentry was standing. No civilians were supposed to be in area. And I saw a truck with a wounded Russian soldier-- young boy, maybe-- was maybe 18, 19 years old, and badly, badly hurt. He was bandaged up, but you could see the blood all over him. And the sentry stopped even that truck. And-- and then-- and then continued-- they were probably trying to figure out where to go. There was a hospital in the area there. So I'm not sure whether he went to the hospital or continued back east.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
